title: Sources of Innovation & Opportunity Recognition
simple_html:
Where do good innovation ideas come from, and how do entrepreneurs spot the ones worth chasing? This lesson answers both: the sources of innovation and the skill of opportunity recognition.

Where innovation comes from

Ideas don't only come from R&D labs. Major sources include:

  • Customers and users — especially "lead users" who hit problems first and hack their own solutions (Eric von Hippel).
  • Changes in the world — Peter Drucker's seven sources: the unexpected, mismatches, process needs, industry/market shifts, demographics, changes in perception, and new knowledge.
  • Suppliers, competitors, employees, and universities.

Spotting opportunities

An opportunity is more than an idea — it's a chance to create value that is desirable, feasible, and timely. Entrepreneurs with an entrepreneurial mindset notice these because of their prior knowledge, alertness, and willingness to act.

Two ways to think: causation vs. effectuation

Saras Sarasvathy found expert entrepreneurs often reason backwards from the usual MBA logic. Causation = pick a goal, then find the means (classic planning). Effectuation = start with who you are, what you know, and whom you know, then build whatever opportunities those means allow — taking small affordable-loss steps and partnering as you go.

Key takeaways

  • Innovation sources are mostly outside the lab — users, change, and networks.
  • An opportunity must be desirable, feasible, and timely.
  • Prior knowledge + alertness drives recognition.
  • Effectuation (means-driven, affordable loss) suits uncertainty; causation (goal-driven planning) suits known markets.
intermediate_html:
This lesson tackles the "front end" of innovation: identifying where opportunities originate and how entrepreneurs recognise and act on them. It combines Drucker's sources framework, von Hippel's user-innovation, the opportunity-recognition literature, and Sarasvathy's causation-vs-effectuation distinction.

Sources of innovation

Innovation seldom springs from lone-genius invention. Key sources include:

  • Drucker's seven sources (Drucker, 1985): the unexpected (success/failure), incongruities, process needs, industry/market structure change, demographics, changes in perception, and new knowledge. Drucker stressed the first four (internal, lower-risk) as more reliable than the last three.
  • Users and lead users (von Hippel, 1986): users — particularly lead users who face needs ahead of the market and benefit from solving them — are a prolific source of innovation, often prototyping solutions firms later commercialise (mountain bikes, surgical equipment).
  • Networks: suppliers, customers, competitors, universities, and employees — anticipating Open Innovation (Lesson 7).

Opportunity recognition

An opportunity is a situation in which new goods/services can be introduced to create value (Shane & Venkataraman, 2000). A robust opportunity is desirable (real customer need/value), feasible (technically and operationally achievable), and timely/durable (a genuine window). Why do some people see opportunities others miss? Two drivers dominate: prior knowledge (you recognise what relates to what you already know — Shane, 2000) and entrepreneurial alertness (Kirzner's notion of being attuned to overlooked profit possibilities). The entrepreneurial mindset combines alertness with bias for action and tolerance of ambiguity.

Jobs-to-be-done

A practical recognition lens is Jobs-to-be-Done (Christensen): customers "hire" products to make progress on a job in a given context. Studying the job — functional, emotional, social — surfaces opportunities that demographic segmentation misses (the classic "milkshake" study reframed a breakfast commute job).

Causation vs. effectuation

Sarasvathy's (2001) study of expert entrepreneurs identified two logics. Causation takes a given goal and selects means to achieve it — the predictive, plan-then-execute logic of textbook strategy ("to the extent we can predict the future, we can control it"). Effectuation inverts this: start from your means — who you are (identity), what you know (knowledge), whom you know (networks) — and let goals emerge. Its four principles are: affordable loss (commit only what you can afford to lose, not expected-return maximisation), crazy-quilt (build partnerships and pre-commitments to co-create the venture), lemonade (leverage surprises and contingencies rather than avoid them), and pilot-in-the-plane (treat the future as shaped by human action, not predicted). "To the extent we can control the future, we do not need to predict it."

Worked example

A former nurse notices hospital staff improvising to keep IV lines organised (an incongruity and a process need in Drucker's terms; a clear job-to-be-done). She has relevant prior knowledge (recognition) and is alert to the unmet need. Rather than write a five-year plan for a goal she cannot validate (causation), she applies effectuation: using her savings she can afford to lose, she 3D-prints prototypes, partners with two ward managers she knows (crazy-quilt), and pivots the design when a surprise use-case emerges in paediatrics (lemonade). The opportunity is co-created through action, not forecast.

How to apply it

  • Systematically scan Drucker's seven sources and engage lead users — don't wait for inspiration.
  • Test any opportunity against desirable / feasible / timely before committing.
  • Frame the customer's job-to-be-done, not just their demographics.
  • Under high uncertainty, default to effectuation (affordable loss, partnerships, leveraging surprise); switch to causation as the market becomes predictable.

This front-end feeds the Search phase of Lesson 3 and sets up Lesson 5, where opportunities are turned into testable business models.
full_html:
Opportunity is the conceptual core of entrepreneurship (Shane & Venkataraman, 2000). This lesson examines two intertwined questions: where do innovation opportunities originate, and how do individuals recognise and act on them? It integrates the sources literature (Drucker, von Hippel), the opportunity-recognition and alertness traditions (Kirzner, Shane), the discovery-vs-creation debate, the Jobs-to-be-Done lens, and Sarasvathy's effectuation theory — among the most influential ideas in modern entrepreneurship scholarship.

Sources of innovation

Drucker (1985) catalogued seven sources of innovation opportunity: (1) the unexpected success, failure, or external event; (2) incongruities between reality and assumption; (3) process needs; (4) changes in industry/market structure; (5) demographics; (6) changes in perception, mood and meaning; and (7) new knowledge. He argued — counter to romantic intuition — that the most reliable sources are the first four (internal to an industry, mundane, lower-risk), whereas new-knowledge innovation, though glamorous, has the longest lead times and highest failure rates. Complementing this, von Hippel (1986; 2005) demonstrated empirically that users, especially lead users (those who experience needs ahead of the market and expect high benefit from a solution), are a dominant and under-exploited source — the loci of innovation are distributed across users, suppliers, and manufacturers, not concentrated in corporate R&D. This democratised innovation view foreshadows Open Innovation (Lesson 7).

Opportunity recognition: alertness, prior knowledge, and the individual-opportunity nexus

Shane and Venkataraman (2000) defined the field's distinctive question as the individual-opportunity nexus: why, when, and how some people discover and exploit opportunities. Two explanatory mechanisms recur. Kirzner (1973) emphasised entrepreneurial alertness — a propensity to notice profit opportunities that others, in market disequilibrium, overlook. Shane (2000) showed that prior knowledge (of markets, of customer problems, of ways to serve markets) channels which opportunities a given individual can recognise; the same technological breakthrough spawns different ventures depending on founders' knowledge corridors. A robust opportunity is typically assessed on three criteria: desirability (genuine, valued customer need), feasibility (technical/operational viability), and timeliness/durability (a real and defensible window).

Discovery vs. creation

A foundational debate concerns the ontology of opportunity. The discovery view (Kirzner; Shane) holds that opportunities exist objectively, formed by exogenous shocks, awaiting alert discoverers — implying search, scanning, and analysis. The creation view (Alvarez & Barney, 2007) argues opportunities do not pre-exist but are enacted through entrepreneurs' actions and social construction — implying experimentation and iterative action under genuine (Knightian) uncertainty. This is not merely philosophical: the two views prescribe different methods (planning/search vs. acting/creating), and they map directly onto causation vs. effectuation below.

Jobs-to-be-Done

Christensen and colleagues' Jobs-to-be-Done theory (Christensen et al., 2016) reframes demand: customers "hire" products and services to make progress on a job arising in a particular circumstance, with functional, emotional and social dimensions. The job, not the customer's demographic profile, is the stable unit of analysis and the locus of opportunity. The framework disciplines opportunity recognition by forcing attention onto the progress a customer seeks and the competing alternatives (including non-consumption) they currently use.

Causation and effectuation

Sarasvathy's (2001) cognitive-science study of 27 expert entrepreneurs produced the field's most cited recent theory. Causation processes "take a particular effect as given and focus on selecting between means to create that effect" — the predictive logic of expected-return maximisation, competitive analysis, and planning. Effectuation processes "take a set of means as given and focus on selecting between possible effects that can be created with those means." Effectual logic rests on four principles plus an overarching worldview: affordable loss (limit downside to what is bearable rather than maximise expected upside); crazy-quilt (forge partnerships and stakeholder pre-commitments that shape the venture); lemonade (exploit contingencies and surprises as resources); pilot-in-the-plane (emphasise human agency — the future is made, not found); under the banner non-predictive control: "to the extent we can control the future, we do not need to predict it." Empirically, expertise correlates with effectual reasoning, particularly under uncertainty; causation tends to dominate in predictable, data-rich environments.

Worked example

Sarasvathy's own teaching case: an entrepreneur with a generic "curry-in-a-hurry" food concept. The causal MBA approach commissions market research, segments, targets, and executes a launch plan toward a pre-set revenue goal. The effectual approach starts with means (this founder's cooking skill, a small affordable-loss budget, a friend who owns a venue), runs a pop-up, lets the first paying customers and a chance catering request (lemonade) redefine the business into corporate catering (an emergent, not pre-set, goal), and binds early partners into the model (crazy-quilt). The opportunity is created through committed action — exactly the creation view in operation.

Common mistakes and misconceptions

  • Idea = opportunity. An idea becomes an opportunity only when desirable, feasible, and timely; most fail the test.
  • Over-reliance on planning under uncertainty. Applying causal forecasting to genuinely unpredictable markets wastes resources; effectuation is better matched.
  • Demographic over job thinking. Segmenting by who customers are rather than the job they need done misses real opportunities.
  • Ignoring users. Treating R&D as the sole source and overlooking lead users (von Hippel).
  • Treating effectuation and causation as rivals. They are complementary: effectuate to discover/create, then shift to causation to scale a validated opportunity.

How to apply

  • Run a structured scan across Drucker's seven sources and recruit lead users into ideation.
  • Articulate the customer's job-to-be-done and the desirable/feasible/timely test for each candidate.
  • Match logic to uncertainty: effectual experimentation early, causal planning to scale.
  • Operationalise affordable loss and stakeholder commitments as your early-stage governance.

Connections to the syllabus

This lesson supplies the Search phase of the innovation process (Lesson 3); effectuation's experiment-and-pivot logic flows directly into the Lean Startup (Lesson 5); user-innovation and networks anticipate Open Innovation (Lesson 7); and affordable-loss thinking connects to staged financing (Lesson 8).
